meta.title: Overcoming Ammonia Synthesis Scaling Relations with Plasma-enabled Catalysis
meta.notes: Figure-read values (STY in Fig 5, Fig 4 TOF comparisons) extracted at lower confidence. Author affiliation: University of Notre Dame.

claims.text: Plasma-induced vibrational excitations in N2 decrease N2 dissociation barriers without influencing subsequent reaction steps; this circumvents ammonia synthesis scaling relations.

location.quote:
We postulate that plasma-induced vibrational
excitations in N2 decrease dissociation barriers without influencing subsequent reaction steps.

claims.predicate: model assumes vibrational excitation reduces N2 dissociation barrier by alpha*Ev and leaves hydrogenation steps unchanged
claims.scope: DFT-based microkinetic model of ammonia synthesis

claims.text: Plasma enhancement is predicted to be particularly great on metals that bind nitrogen too weakly to be active thermally.

location.quote:
We predict plasma enhancement to be particularly great on metals that bind nitrogen too
weakly to be active thermally.

claims.predicate: predicted rate enhancement is largest for weak-N-binding metals (right leg of volcano)

claims.text: Ammonia synthesis rates observed in a DBD plasma reactor are consistent with predicted enhancements and with predicted changes in the optimal metal catalyst.

location.quote:
Ammonia synthesis rates observed in a DBD plasma reactor are
consistent with predicted enhancements and with predicted changes in the optimal metal catalyst.

claims.predicate: measured STY trend matches model-predicted volcano ordering/shift

claims.text: Conventional Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis operates at 100-200 atm and 600-800 K; practical rates on best thermal catalysts require ~700 K and ~100 atm.

location.quote:
practical rates are only attainable at elevated temperatures (∼ 700 K)
and pressures (∼ 100 atm)

claims.predicate: stated Haber-Bosch conditions ~700 K, ~100 atm (and 100-200 atm, 600-800 K)

claims.text: The microkinetic model recovers the volcano curve and indicates Ru and Fe are the optimal mono-metallic catalysts at high T and P (673 K, 100 atm).

location.quote:
Our results recover the volcano curve predicted
by literature models [7, 8] and indicate that Ru and Fe are the optimal mono-metallic cat-
alysts at high T and P.

claims.predicate: model volcano peak at high T/P corresponds to Ru and Fe

claims.text: At reduced temperature and pressure, rates on the same stepped surfaces are several orders of magnitude lower than the corresponding Haber-Bosch rates, and terrace rates are a further few orders of magnitude lower.

location.quote:
rates on the same stepped
surfaces are several orders of magnitude lower than the corresponding Haber-Bosch rates.
Rates on terrace sites are a further few orders of magnitude lower.

claims.predicate: log10(TOF) difference between target-condition steps and Haber-Bosch is several orders; terraces further lower
claims.scope: Figure 1, 473 K, 1 atm vs 673 K, 100 atm

claims.text: N2 has a relatively large vibrational spacing of ~0.3 eV.

location.quote: relatively large vibrational spacing (∼ 0.3 eV)

claims.predicate: N2 vibrational spacing ≈ 0.3 eV

claims.text: The bulk gas temperature measured by in situ thermocouple was 398.5 ± 5 K.

location.quote: temperature (Tgas), measured using an in situ thermocouple, was 398.5 ± 5 K.

claims.predicate: Tgas = 398.5 ± 5 K (measured)

claims.text: An average N2 vibrational temperature of Tvib = 3000 K was used in the model; Tvib had weak dependence on gas composition.

location.quote: An average value of Tvib = 3000 K was used in our model.

claims.predicate: model uses Tvib = 3000 K

claims.text: The Treanor distribution at Tvib=3000 K, Tgas=473 K predicts ~63.2% of N2 in the ground state, 21.4% in the first excited state, and ~0.2% in tail states v8-v10.

location.quote:
only about 63.2 % of N2 remains in the ground vibrational state. The first vibrationally
excited state has a population of 21.4 %, while the states in the tail of the distribution (v8 -
v10) have probabilites of around 0.2 %.

claims.scope: Figure 3b, Tvib=3000 K, Tgas=473 K, first 10 excited states

claims.text: Rates on the optimal plasma-on catalyst are comparable to those on the optimal thermal catalyst (Ru steps) at 673 K and 100 atm.

location.quote:
rates on the optimal plasma-on catalyst are
comparable to those on the optimal thermal catalyst at 673 K and 100 atm, Ru steps.

claims.predicate: peak plasma-on TOF (473 K, 1 atm) ≈ peak Haber-Bosch TOF (Ru steps, 673 K, 100 atm)

claims.text: For step sites the plasma-on volcano peak shifts towards weaker nitrogen binding, predicting Co and Ni near the peak.

location.quote:
the peak of the plasma-on volcano curve shows
a distinct shift towards weaker nitrogen binding energies, predicting Co and Ni to be near
the volcano peak.

claims.predicate: plasma-on step volcano peak located at Co/Ni binding energies

claims.text: Plasma-on TOF on Ru terrace sites is the same order of magnitude as that on Ru steps, despite plasma-off TOFs five orders of magnitude apart; Ru terrace TOF predicted two orders of magnitude below Co steps.

location.quote:
the plasma-on TOF on Ru terrace sites is of the same order of magnitude of
that on Ru steps, despite the fact that their plasma-off TOFs are five orders of magnitude
apart.

claims.predicate: plasma-on TOF(Ru terrace) ~ plasma-on TOF(Ru step); plasma-off difference ~5 orders; Ru terrace ~2 orders below Co steps

claims.text: The observed experimental activity trend Co > Ni = Ru > Pt > Fe tracks well with model predictions.

location.quote:
the observed activity trend of Co > Ni = Ru > Pt
> Fe tracks remarkably well with model predictions.

claims.predicate: measured STY ordering Co > Ni = Ru > Pt > Fe consistent with model volcano ordering

claims.text: Measured ammonia site-time yields: Co ~0.063, Ru ~0.027, Ni ~0.027, Pt ~0.017, Fe ~0.010 s^-1.

location.quote:
Measured ammonia site-time yields in the di-
electric barrier discharge reactor on metal/Al2O3 catalysts as a function of the DFT-calculated
nitrogen adsorption energy.

claims.predicate: STY values read from Figure 5: Co≈0.063, Ru≈0.027, Ni≈0.027, Pt≈0.017, Fe≈0.010 s^-1
claims.scope: Figure 5, N2:H2=2:1, plasma power=10 W, 438 K

claims.text: Non-stoichiometric N2:H2 ratios in the range 1:1 to 3:1 produced higher ammonia yields than the stoichiometric 1:3 ratio.

location.quote:
non-stoichiometric ratios in the range of 1:1 to
3:1 produced higher ammonia yields than the stoichiometric 1:3 ratio

claims.predicate: ammonia yield at N2:H2 in [1:1,3:1] > yield at 1:3

claims.text: The DBD plasma was operated at 10 W and ~20 kHz at atmospheric pressure with reactor temperature maintained at 438 K; catalysts verified thermally inactive.

location.quote:
The DBD plasma was operated at 10 W and ∼ 20 kHz at atmospheric
pressure, and the reactor temperature maintained at 438 K. The catalysts were verified as
thermally inactive under these conditions.

claims.predicate: plasma power=10 W, freq~20 kHz, T=438 K, atmospheric pressure

claims.text: Five metal catalysts (Fe, Ru, Co, Ni, Pt) supported on gamma-alumina with 5 wt.% metal loading were compared.

location.quote:
We compared five different metal catalysts (Fe, Ru, Co, Ni, Pt)
supported on γ-alumina

claims.predicate: 5 catalysts Fe,Ru,Co,Ni,Pt on gamma-alumina, 5 wt% metal

claims.text: The reactor configuration yields a discharge gap of 1.75 mm and a discharge volume of ~0.18 mL.

location.quote: configuration yields a discharge gap of 1.75 mm and a discharge volume of ∼ 0.18 mL.

claims.predicate: discharge gap = 1.75 mm, discharge volume ≈ 0.18 mL

claims.text: Vibrational excitation has significantly lower excitation energy thresholds than other forms of excitation and is the predominant excitation channel for N2 in non-thermal plasmas.

location.quote:
Vibrational excitation
has significantly lower excitation energy thresholds compared to other forms of excitation
(e.g. electronic excitation, ionization, or dissociation), and is thus the predominant ex-
citation channel for N2 in non-thermal plasmas [21].

claims.predicate: vibrational excitation threshold < electronic/ionization/dissociation; dominant N2 channel

claims.text: Ammonia was the only product observed (no hydrazine or other N-containing products).

location.quote:
ammonia was the only product observed (i.e. no hydrazine
or other N-containing products).

claims.predicate: only product detected by GC-TCD is NH3

claims.text: The DFT calculations used the DACAPO code with the RPBE functional, Vanderbilt ultrasoft pseudopotentials, and a plane-wave cutoff of at least 340 eV.

location.quote:
all calculations were performed using the DACAPO
code, with exchange and correlation effects described by the RPBE functional [47]. Core
electrons were described using Vanderbilt ultrasoft pseudopotentials. The plane-wave cutoff
used was at least 340 eV.

claims.predicate: DFT: DACAPO, RPBE, ultrasoft PP, cutoff >= 340 eV
